text: In this video I'll be taking you through how I like to perform a robotic umbilical hernia repair with mesh using the tap technique or the transabdominal preperitoneal technique. This is a mesh and sutures I'll be using for this case.

Positioning is very important. I usually do use three trocars of eight millimeters in size. It's important to have a little bit of flex in the bed and also I like to push the legs slightly away from the trocars to get a little more access. in the pelvis to avoid collisions with the arm.

The surgeon uses an OptiView technique with a robotic eight millimeter trocar to access the peritoneum, achieving insufflation to fifteen millimeters of mercury. A small umbilical defect is visible with a portion of incarcerated fat. Additional trocars are placed under direct visualization.

and once we have the trocars in place and we can go ahead and start to dock our robot it's important to have everyone on the team being on the same page to quickly dock and maneuver for setup and every second really counts to make sure you have an efficient team I usually do have my assistant do the positioning of the robot while I get ready at the console and again I like to trim off as much waste in the time of this as possible in order to make sure things are moving smoothly and efficiently so once the robot is docked at this point I prepare for the console.

A small umbilical hernia is visualized. The surgeon uses hot scissors with electrocautery and a pro grasp to begin the procedure. A wide peritoneal flap, approximately ten centimeters in diameter, is created to provide ample space for the mesh placement, tailored to the size of the hernia defect.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using scissors, sparingly applying electrocautery to preserve muscle fibers and avoid excessive injury. The dissection occurs in a fine plane above the peritoneum, aiming to avoid penetrating the umbilical hernia sac. The robotic 3D optics enhance visualization, showing the defect opening up with minimal bleeding.
The surgeon separates the skin from the hernia sac, revealing a clear defect in the fascia at the umbilicus. The video is sped up to show this process more quickly.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect, measuring approximately two centimeters. A nine-centimeter mesh is opened for placement over the defect. The TAP approach is considered to preserve anatomical integrity and prevent mesh migration.

The robotic system provides a clear view of the anatomy. The surgeon uses a pro grasp instrument, with tips measuring approximately two centimeters, as a measuring device. Sutures are being passed to close the peritoneum, starting slightly away from the defect. The suture is locked in place, and several throws are made to ensure a secure closure.

The surgeon begins closing the fascial defect using a PDS suture, reducing intra-abdominal pressure to 12 mmHg to facilitate closure. The suture is tightened after each throw, and a second layer is prepared to reinforce the closure by returning to the initial suture entry point.

The surgeon confirms the complete closure of the hernia defect and ensures no lipoma is trapped inside by having the assistant push out any air. The coated mesh is unrolled and positioned with the coating facing the bowel to prevent direct contact with intra-abdominal organs. An absorbable two-arm suture is used to secure the mesh, avoiding the need for laparoscopic tacks.

The surgeon uses a plier to suture the mesh in place on one side, employing a double arm suture technique to secure the remainder of the mesh. The CT-one needle is used for fascial closure, with sutures available in various lengths, including a 24x24 cm bidirectional suture for mesh fixation.

The surgeon finalizes the closure of the peritoneum, ensuring the mesh is securely fixed in place and separated from the small bowel. The mesh is sandwiched between the patient's peritoneum to minimize future adhesions. The sutures are tied down and removed to complete the procedure.

usually takes about a half hour of time again by limiting the steps by having reproducibility in the OR having good assistance I think you can have a lot of time saving and cost savings and I think ultimately by avoiding mesh tackers, the cost savings to the operating room, to the patients is very beneficial.

This patient did very well, was discharged home several hours later and had a very uneventful post-operative course. Another thing we like to routinely do is place compression dressing on these patients. We just use a small piece of a four by four and a tegaderm. We do use a needle to create a vacuum here. And I think this can potentially decrease the dead space of the area and perhaps prevent a seroma. The needle is put in. And we hook it up to suction, which does create a tight vacuum seal. This dressing is taken down three days later.
